California
Fair P01itical
Practices Commission
ApriI 10, 199 0
David H. Hirsch
city Attorney
city of Lompoc
city HaI1
100 civic center Plaza
P. O. Box 8 001
Lompoc, CA 93438-8001
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-90-195
Dear Mr. Ilirsch:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Lompoc city councilmenbers christa Marks and J.D. snith
concerning their duties under the conflic!-of-interest 
provisions
of the Potitical Reform Act (the tta6gr1.1/
OUESTTONS
1. May councilmeDbers lIarks and Surith participate in the
consideration of the proposed development of Property that is rnore
than 2,500 feet from real property they own?
2. Ii{ay councilmenbers Marks and Snith participate in the
consideration of amenduents to the existing zoning ordinance for
the City of Lonpoc which involve proposed changes in the uses
perroitted or developnent standards applicable to the various zon-
ing categories?
CONCLUSIONS
1. Councilmenbers ltarks and Suith nay participate in the
consideration of the proposed development of property that is more
than 2,500 feet froD real property they own.
2. councilnenbers llarks and Surith nay participate in the
consideration of anendnents to the existing zoning ordinance for
L/ covernment Code Sections 810OO-91015. ALL statutory refer-
ences are to the covernment Code unless otherwise indicated. Con-
mission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, e,E seq, AlL references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the california Code of Regulations.
Street, suite 8oo . P.o. Box 8o7 o Sacramento cA 95go4.o8oz . (916) 3 z2.s66o
428 J


FiIe No. A-90-196
Paqe 2
the City of Lompoc which involve proposed changes in the uses
pernitted or developuent standards applicable to the various zon-
ing categorj.es, provided the decisions will not foreseeably
increase or decrease the fair market value of their real property
by $10,000 or nore or the rental value of their property by S1,0O0
in a 12-month period.
FAqTS
The LoErpoc City Councit will be considering the fo1lo$ring
issues:
1. A development proposal concerning a 10f5O0 square foot
lot with two existing residences. The owner has asked for ap-
proval to add a third residence on the lot and subdivide the
property into separate properties for ownership purposes. Because
the lot is currently zoned for mediun density residential uses
(R-2) this project e/ould only be pennitted if the property is
rezoned as a planned development district 
(PD).
2. Zoning Amendments.
a. The planned developrnent district 
zoning category
pernits the development of property to accomrnodate various
tl4)es of developnent, such as shopping centers, professional
and adrninistrative offices and residential, units.
Horrever, questions have been raised by nembers of the
public concerning the propriety of using the PD zoningt
designation on sma1l lots in existing neighborhoods. In ad-
dition, the public has become concerned about the potential
of such projects for changing the character of existing
residentiat areas. Thus, the city council will be reviewing
the appropriateness of using this zoning overlay technique in
existing neighborhoods and possible nodifications to the
designation. In addition, the city council will consider a
variety of technica.L anendments to the ordinance as applied
to PD zones.
b, The city council will be considering a variety of
issues concerning nediun density residential zones ,
including whether free standing and attached single-fanily
dwellings, and nuLti-farnily dwellings such as duplexes and
triplexes should be pennitted, and rrhether condj.tional use
pernits should be required for such developnent.
c. Questions have been raised regarding city staff,s
nanner of interpreting the zoning ordinance with respect to
the rnaxinun density in the R-2 zone. The ordinance requires
that no less than 3,000 square feet of land area be aviilable
for each dwelling unit. 
staff has interpreted the calcula-
tlon of the nunber of units that wil1 be allowed on the lot
based upon neeting density reguj.renents in relation to the
size of the Iot (i.e., a 9,ooo square foot 1ot could have
three units, using the 3,OOO square feet of land per dwelling


FiIe No. A-90-196
Page 3
unit calculation). 
The interpretation is being presented to
the city council for confirmation or modif j-cation.
d. Questions have also been raised regarding city
staff,s interpretation of the zoning ordinance's requirement
that there be a 2o-foot separation between residential dwell-
ings. The language of the ordinance is anbiguous and the
staff will be seeking gmidance from the city council as to
how the requirement should be inplemented.
e. The zoninq ordinance also requires th.at accessory
buildings be attached to I'the main building. " A question has
been raised as to whether the nain buildj.ng refers to one or
any nain building on a specific lot. 
staff has interpreted
this section to require that accessory buildings have a con-
non roof with ratr urain building as oPposed to "the main
building." 
Staff will be seeking guidance frorn the city
council as to how the requirement should be irnplenented.
councilnembers snith and Marks both own property in the city
of Lompoc. councilnember l.tarks olrns two propertj,es, one zoned
rnediurn density residential (R-3) $rhich currently contains her
residence and two rentaL units. 
In addition, councilmember D1arks
owns property zoned R-2 $rhich contains four rentaL units.
councilDember snith owns property zoned R-2 s/hich is currently
developed with three units. Al1 the properties ot ned by
Councilnenbers }larks and Snith are more than 2,500 feet a\{ay from
the property subject to the devetoPment decision.
ANALYSTS
Section 8710o prohibits any public official 
frorn making,
participating in naking, or otherwise using hj,s or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the of-
ficial has a financial interest. 
Section 87103 specifies that an
official 
has a financial interest within the meaning of Section
87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision $rill have
a naterial financial effect, distingruishable from the effect on
the public generally, on the official or a nenber of his or her
innediate fanily or on:
Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth one
thousand dollars (51,000) or nore.
section 87103 (b) .
City councilnenbers are public officials. 
(Section 82048.)
Iloreover, both council,Denbers have investnents i.n their real
property of S1,000 or more. Thus, the counci lmenbers, real,
property interests are potentj,ally disquallfying financial
interests as defined in Section 87103.
However, Section 87103 specifies that a public oificial 
has a
financial. interest in a decision only if it ii reasonabrv foresee-
able that the decision will have a rnaterial financiE1 EfEEEe


File No. A-9o-195
Page 4
distinguishable froD the effect on the public aenerally, on the
official,s 
financial interest. 
whether the financial consequences
of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the time a
governmental decision is made depends on the facts of each
particular case. An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable
if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur.
certainty is not required. However, if an effect is only a mere
possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner
(1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198, copy enclosed.)
The DeveloDment Proposal
The developnent proposal involves property that is more than
2,50o feet frorn the property interests of the counc i lrnernbers .
Regulation ]-a702.3 (b) (copy enclosed) provides:
(b) The reasonably foreseeable effect of a
decision is not considered materiaL as to real
property in which an official 
has a direct,
indirect or beneficial interest (not including a
leasehotd interest), if the real property in which
the official 
has an interest is located entirely
beyond a 2,5oo foot radius of the boundaries (or
the proposed boundaries) of the Property which is
the subject of the decision; unless:
(1) There are specific circumstances
regarding the decision, its effect, and the
nature of the real property in which the of-
ficial has an interest, which nake it reason-
ably foreseeable that the fair market value or
the rental value of the real- property in which
the official 
has an interest will be affected
by the amounts set forth in subdivisions
(a) (3) (A) or (a) (3) (B); and
(2) Either of the following aPPIY:
(A) The effect will not be
substantially the saBe as the effect upon
at least 25 percent of al,L the properties
which are within a 2,5oo foot radius of
the boundaries of the real property in
which the official 
has an interest,' or
(B) There are not at least 10
properties under separate ovnership
within a 2,500 foot radius of the
property in which the officiaL has an
interest.
Thus, absent special circunstances which nake lt reasonably
foreseeable that the fair rnarket value of the real property will
be affected, Regnrlation ta7o2.3(b) would perDit both
councilmenbers to participate in the developnent decision. your


FiIe No. A-90-196
Page 5
facts indicate no special circumstances suggesting that the deci-
sion regarding the development will have a foreseeable financial
effect on a property so far removed from the subject property.
While concern has been raised by the public about potential
rrprecedential effects[ from the decision, absent sone indication
that the councj.Imembers have taken objective steps toward utj.liz-
ing the precedent, it would appear that any financial effects fron
the decision on the councilmembers are too speculative at this
tirne. Consequently, both Councilnember !{arks and Snith may
participate in the decision concerning the developnent project.
The ceneral zoninq Decisions
In addition, the city council will be considerj.ng a variety
of changes to the Lompoc zoning ordinance applicable generally to
R-2 and R-3 properties in the city. 
Both councilnember Marks and
Councj-lroember sDith own R-2 properties. In addition,
councilnember l,[arks owns property zoned R-3.
Generally, lrhere a governnental decision concerns zoning or
rezoning, and a public official resides in the zone, the effect of
the decision is deemed naterial and the official 
nay not
participate. 
(Regulation 18702.1(a) (3) (A), copy enclosed.)
However, Regnrlation La702.1(a) (3) (E) defines rrzoningrr decisions to
exclude amendments to an existing zoning ordinance which are ap-
plicable to all properties designated in that category.
According to the facts you provided, the city council will be
confronted with issues involving both the interpretation of exist-
ing portions of Lompoc's zoning ordinance and the aDendment of
various provisions of the ordinance. since these decisions
involve changes within the definition of particular zoning
categories which vi1l be applicable to aII the properties
designated in that category, we conclude that the decisions faI1
within the exenption provided by subdivision (E) of Regulation
18702.1. Thus, under the facts presented, Regulation
lg7o2.1(a) (3) (A) does not apply to the councj.luenbers, properties
i.nvolved in the decision.
However′  the dete.ttination of whether a conf■
ict of interest
exists does not end with the app■
ication of 
■8702。■(a)(3)(E)。
Regulation 
■8702(a) (cOpy enclosed)proVides:
In order to dete.ttine if a decision′
s effect is
IIlateria■′ it must first be dete■
■uined if the
officia■′s economic interest is directly involved
and the effect of the decision is materia■
 under
Section 18702.■.  If the official′ s economic ittLer―
est is not directlv involved in the dec■
s■on. or
the effect of the dec■
s■on ■s not mater■al. under
Section ■8702.■. then it must be dete.`uined if the
of Sections ■8702.2 throucrh ■8702.6.
Emphas■s added.


File No. A-90-196
Page 6
Thus, the effects of the decisions nust still 
be analyzed
under the standards if Regulation 18702.3 to determine if the
indirect effect on the official's 
property is significant enough
to result in disqual if icat ion.
Regulation La702.3 (c) states that for decisions which may
affect an interest in real property but lrhich do not involve a
subject property fron which the distances can be deternined, the
monetary standards contained in 18702.3(a) (3) (A) and (B) shall be
applied. Regulation 18702.3(a) (3) provides that the effect of a
decision on real property in which an official 
has an econonic
j.nterest is rnaterial if the decision will have a reasonabl,y
foreseeable financial effect of:
(A) Ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or nore on
the fair rnarket value of the real ProPerty in vhich
the official 
has an interest; or
(B) will affect the rental value of the
property by 9f,ooo or nore per 12 nonth period.
Consequently, the councilrnembers Day participate in the
general zonj.nq decisions despite the fact that the changes will be
directly applj.cable to their properties. However, they nay not
participate in the zoning decisions if the decisions will
ioreseeibly increase or decrease the fair market value of their
real property by $10,ooo or Dore or the rental value of their
property by $1,ooo in a 12-nonth peri-od. (Regulation
L87 02.3 (a) (3). )
If you find that any of the zoning decisions are likely to
affect the councilnembers' respective properties by the amount set
out in 1a7o2.3 (a) (3), the councilmembers may still 
participate if
the effect on their property is not distinguishable from the ef-
fect on the public generally. For the |tpublic generallyrr excep-
tion to apply, a decision must affect the official's 
interests in
substantiallv the same rnanner as it rrould affect a significant
segment of the public. (Regulation 18703, copy enclosed.)
The rrpublicrr consists of the entire jurisdiction of the
agency in guestion. (In re or.ren (1976, 2 FPPC ops. 77, coPy
enclosed.) This is so because all the residents of the jurisdic-
tion are constituents of the official. 
(fn re Leoan (1985) 9 FPPC
ops. 1, copy enclosed.) Consequently, for the rrpublic aenerallyt'
exception to apply to this situation, the zoning decision nust
affect a signiflcant segTroent of the population of Lonpoc in
substantially the same nanner as it t/ould affect the
councilnembers. (Dowd Advice Letter, No. A-88-214; Burnham Advice
Letter, No. A-86-210, copies enclosed.)
For example, if a decision rri11 affect a public official,s
property by $10,000 or nore, but r,i1I affect a large portion of
the city to the sane extent, the public generally exception would
be applicable. However, if the decision wilt affect the public


I.ile No. A-90-195
Page 7
official's 
property by S1o,00o, and al1 the other properties in
the jurisdiction to a lesser extent, it would appear a significant
segment would not be affected as the public official, 
and the
public generally exception $rould not apply.
I trust that this answers your questions. If you have any
further questions regarding this matter, please feel free to
contact this office at (916) 322-59oL.
Sincerely,
Kathryn E. Donovan
Counse 1
By:
wal lace
, Legal Division

agency in guestion. (In re or.ren (1976, 2 FPPC ops. 77, coPy
enclosed.) This is so because all the residents of the jurisdic-
tion are constituents of the official. 
(fn re Leoan (1985) 9 FPPC
ops. 1, copy enclosed.) Consequently, for the rrpublic aenerallyt'
exception to apply to this situation, the zoning decision nust
affect a signiflcant segTroent of the population of Lonpoc in
substantially the same nanner as it t/ould affect the
councilnembers. (Dowd Advice Letter, No. A-88-214; Burnham Advice
Letter, No. A-86-210, copies enclosed.)
For example, if a decision rri11 affect a public official,s
property by $10,000 or nore, but r,i1I affect a large portion of
the city to the sane extent, the public generally exception would
be applicable. However, if the decision wilt affect the public


I.ile No. A-90-195
official's 
property by S1o,00o, and al1 the other properties in
the jurisdiction to a lesser extent, it would appear a significant
segment would not be affected as the public official, 
and the
public generally exception $rould not apply.
I trust that this answers your questions. If you have any
further questions regarding this matter, please feel free to
contact this office at (916) 322-59oL.

d. Questions have also been raised regarding city

e. The zoninq ordinance also requires th.at accessory
